review.note: AI-drafted first pass - SME review required before production

title: Explorers from Europe Find Southern Africa
subject: History
caps_topic: Explorers from Europe find southern Africa

meta.title: Explorers from Europe Find Southern Africa

sources.title: Bartolomeu Dias reaches present Mossel Bay
Portuguese navigator Bartholomew Dias erects the first stone cross on the South African coast
Explorer and navigator Vasco da Gama completes his voyage round the Cape of Good Hope to India
Van Riebeeck lands at the Cape
Jan van Riebeeck, Dutch colonial administrator and first commander of the colony at the Cape of Good Hope, is born

slides.title: Explorers from Europe Find Southern Africa
slides.lead: How Portuguese and Dutch sailors reached the tip of Africa, and what their long voyages meant for the people already living here.

media.alt: A wooden sailing ship on blue ocean waves next to the green coast of southern Africa, under a bright sun.
media.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 320 180' role='img'><rect width='320' height='180' fill='#f4efe6'/><rect y='118' width='320' height='62' fill='#3a6b8a'/><path d='M0 118 q40 -10 80 0 t80 0 t80 0 t80 0 v62 H0 Z' fill='#2f5a78'/><circle cx='262' cy='42' r='24' fill='#e8a13a'/><path d='M40 118 L64 108 h96 l24 10 Z' fill='#7a5a3a'/><rect x='108' y='58' width='4' height='60' fill='#5c3f26'/><path d='M112 60 q40 8 0 46 Z' fill='#f4efe6'/><path d='M112 60 q-38 8 0 46 Z' fill='#dcd3c2'/></svg>

slides.notes: Open by asking learners what an 'explorer' is and why people long ago sailed into oceans they did not know. Set up the two big ideas for the lesson: (1) sailors from Portugal and then the Netherlands (Holland) found a sea route around southern Africa, and (2) their arrival changed life for the Khoikhoi and San who already lived here. Remind learners that 'finding' a place does not mean it was empty - people had lived here for thousands of years.

slides.title: Why did Europeans want to sail south?
slides.lead: They were looking for a sea road to the rich trade of the East.

blocks.text: Long ago, spices like pepper and cinnamon, along with silk and gold, came to Europe all the way from India and the East. They travelled slowly over land, so they were very **expensive**.

items: European kings wanted a faster, cheaper way to reach the East by sea.
The people of **Portugal** decided to sail south along the coast of Africa to find that route.
Nobody in Europe yet knew if ships could sail around the bottom of Africa.

media.alt: Small bags and jars of spices with coins beside them, showing valuable trade goods.
media.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 220 160' role='img'><rect width='220' height='160' fill='#f4efe6'/><path d='M40 120 q10 -46 30 -46 q20 0 30 46 Z' fill='#8a5a2a'/><path d='M96 120 q9 -40 26 -40 q17 0 26 40 Z' fill='#b5732f'/><ellipse cx='70' cy='76' rx='18' ry='6' fill='#5c3a17'/><ellipse cx='122' cy='82' rx='16' ry='6' fill='#7a4a1f'/><circle cx='168' cy='104' r='14' fill='#e8a13a'/><circle cx='184' cy='112' r='12' fill='#d18f2c'/><circle cx='176' cy='94' r='10' fill='#f0b24a'/></svg>

slides.notes: Keep this concrete: the reason for these dangerous voyages was money and trade. Spices were used to flavour and keep food, and were worth a lot. Explain that at this time Europeans had to buy Eastern goods through many traders, which made them costly - a direct sea route would let Portugal trade for itself. Do not overload learners with names yet; the explorers come next.

slides.title: The people already living here
slides.lead: Southern Africa was home to the Khoikhoi and the San long before any European ship arrived.

blocks.text: The **San** were hunter-gatherers who hunted animals and gathered plants. The **Khoikhoi** kept herds of cattle and sheep and moved to find fresh grass and water. Together they are often called the **Khoisan**.

items: They had lived in southern Africa for thousands of years.
The Khoikhoi near the coast met the first European sailors face to face.
This land was their home - it was not empty or waiting to be 'found'.

media.alt: Two cattle grazing on green land near a domed grass shelter, showing Khoikhoi herders.
media.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 240 160' role='img'><rect width='240' height='160' fill='#f4efe6'/><rect y='108' width='240' height='52' fill='#7a8b5a'/><path d='M30 108 q26 -30 52 0 Z' fill='#c8a25a'/><path d='M40 108 v-12 h4 v12 Z' fill='#8a6a34'/><g fill='#5c3a1f'><ellipse cx='150' cy='96' rx='22' ry='12'/><rect x='134' y='96' width='4' height='16'/><rect x='160' y='96' width='4' height='16'/><path d='M130 90 q-6 -6 -2 -10' stroke='#5c3a1f' stroke-width='3' fill='none'/></g><g fill='#7a5a3a'><ellipse cx='196' cy='104' rx='16' ry='9'/><rect x='185' y='104' width='3' height='12'/><rect x='203' y='104' width='3' height='12'/></g></svg>

slides.notes: This slide matters for a fair and honest history. Make clear that people had lived here for a very long time with their own ways of life. Distinguish gently: San were hunter-gatherers, Khoikhoi were herders with cattle and sheep. The word 'discover' or 'find' is used from the European point of view; the Khoisan already knew this land as home. This sets up 'first contact' later.

slides.title: Portugal leads the way
slides.lead: Step by step, Portuguese sailors mapped the west coast of Africa.

blocks.text: Sailors from **Portugal** did not sail all the way at once. On each voyage they went a little further south down the African coast and came home to share what they had learned.

items: They sailed in strong wooden ships called **caravels** that could sail against the wind.
Each captain added new coastline to Portugal's maps.
Their goal was still to find the far end of Africa and sail on to the East.

media.alt: A caravel sailing ship with three masts and full white sails on the sea.
media.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 220 160' role='img'><rect width='220' height='160' fill='#f4efe6'/><rect y='118' width='220' height='42' fill='#3a6b8a'/><path d='M46 118 L70 106 h84 l22 12 Z' fill='#7a5a3a'/><g stroke='#5c3f26' stroke-width='3'><line x1='78' y1='40' x2='78' y2='106'/><line x1='112' y1='30' x2='112' y2='106'/><line x1='146' y1='44' x2='146' y2='106'/></g><path d='M80 44 q26 6 0 40 Z' fill='#f4efe6'/><path d='M114 34 q30 8 0 48 Z' fill='#dcd3c2'/><path d='M148 48 q22 6 0 36 Z' fill='#f4efe6'/><rect x='110' y='30' width='16' height='10' fill='#8a1f1f'/></svg>

slides.notes: Explain that exploration was a slow, careful, step-by-step process over many years, not one single trip. Introduce the caravel: a light, fast ship that could sail against the wind, which made long ocean voyages possible. Keep names light here - the two famous captains (Dias and da Gama) get their own slides next.

slides.eyebrow: How did they not get lost?
slides.title: Finding the way at sea
slides.lead: With no maps of the open ocean, sailors used the sun, the stars and simple tools.

blocks.text: Far out at sea there are no roads or road signs. Explorers used a **compass** to find north, and they watched the **sun and stars** to work out where they were.

items: A tool called an **astrolabe** helped measure how high the sun or a star sat in the sky.
The Portuguese planted large stone crosses, called **padrao**, on the coast to mark how far they had reached.
Voyages were dangerous - storms, hunger and sickness could strike far from home.

media.alt: A round compass showing north, next to a tall stone cross planted on the shore.
media.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 220 160' role='img'><rect width='220' height='160' fill='#f4efe6'/><circle cx='72' cy='80' r='40' fill='#dcd3c2' stroke='#5c3f26' stroke-width='3'/><path d='M72 46 L82 80 L72 114 L62 80 Z' fill='#8a1f1f'/><path d='M72 80 L82 80' stroke='#5c3f26' stroke-width='2'/><text x='72' y='40' font-size='11' text-anchor='middle' fill='#5c3f26'>N</text><rect x='150' y='40' width='10' height='84' fill='#b7a98d'/><rect x='134' y='58' width='42' height='10' fill='#b7a98d'/><rect x='128' y='120' width='54' height='14' fill='#7a8b5a'/></svg>

slides.notes: This is a hands-on, curious slide - Grade 6 learners enjoy the 'how'. Explain simply that the compass needle always points north, and that the height of the sun or stars told sailors how far north or south they were. Introduce 'padrao' (stone cross) because Dias planted one and it appears on the next slide. Stress the real danger to build respect for how hard these journeys were.

slides.title: Bartolomeu Dias rounds the Cape
slides.lead: In 1488 a Portuguese captain became the first European known to sail around the tip of Africa.

blocks.text: **Bartolomeu Dias** sailed so far south that a storm blew his ships around the bottom of Africa without him even seeing land. He had rounded the **Cape**, proving that ships could reach the East by sea.

items: He landed at Mossel Bay and planted a stone cross on the coast.
He first called the point the 'Cape of Storms' because of the wild weather.
The King of Portugal renamed it the **Cape of Good Hope**, because now there was hope of reaching India.

media.alt: A ship being pushed by strong wind and waves around a rocky cape, with a stone cross on the headland.
media.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 240 160' role='img'><rect width='240' height='160' fill='#f4efe6'/><rect y='110' width='240' height='50' fill='#3a6b8a'/><path d='M0 110 q30 -14 60 0 t60 0 t60 0 t60 0 v50 H0 Z' fill='#2f5a78'/><path d='M150 110 q40 -30 90 -20 v20 Z' fill='#6b6152'/><rect x='196' y='58' width='7' height='34' fill='#b7a98d'/><rect x='186' y='68' width='27' height='7' fill='#b7a98d'/><path d='M48 108 L68 98 h44 l16 10 Z' fill='#7a5a3a'/><line x1='84' y1='60' x2='84' y2='98' stroke='#5c3f26' stroke-width='3'/><path d='M86 62 q30 8 0 34 Z' fill='#f4efe6'/><path d='M20 40 q30 8 60 2' stroke='#9aa77f' stroke-width='3' fill='none'/></svg>

slides.notes: This is one of the two key voyages of the lesson. Emphasise 1488 and the idea of a 'first' from the European record. Tell the story simply: a storm carried Dias around the Cape almost by accident, which proved the sea route was possible. The name change from 'Cape of Storms' to 'Cape of Good Hope' is a memorable detail - ask learners why 'good hope' was a good name. [SME] Please confirm the wording that Dias landed at Mossel Bay and the naming of the Cape against the SAHO article before production.

slides.title: Vasco da Gama reaches India
slides.lead: About ten years later, another Portuguese captain sailed all the way to India and back.

blocks.text: **Vasco da Gama** set out in 1497. His ships stopped along the southern African coast for fresh water and rest, then sailed up the east coast and across to **India**, which he reached in 1498.

items: He named the east coast **Natal**, because he sailed past it near Christmas.
He opened the full sea route from Europe to the East.
Now Portuguese ships passed the Cape often on their way to trade.

media.alt: A simple map showing a dotted sailing route from Europe down around Africa and across to India.
media.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 240 160' role='img'><rect width='240' height='160' fill='#3a6b8a'/><path d='M70 20 q-16 40 8 70 q18 22 0 46 q-6 12 14 12 q26 0 30 -22' fill='#7a8b5a'/><path d='M150 24 q22 30 6 66 q-10 22 8 40' fill='#c8b89a'/><path d='M60 26 C40 60 70 96 78 120 C82 138 120 138 150 120' fill='none' stroke='#f4efe6' stroke-width='3' stroke-dasharray='6 5'/><circle cx='60' cy='26' r='5' fill='#8a1f1f'/><circle cx='150' cy='120' r='5' fill='#e8a13a'/><text x='60' y='16' font-size='10' fill='#f4efe6' text-anchor='middle'>Portugal</text><text x='176' y='118' font-size='10' fill='#f4efe6' text-anchor='middle'>India</text></svg>

slides.notes: The second key voyage. Da Gama finished what Dias started: an open sea road all the way to India. Point out on the map how the route hugs the African coast and rounds the Cape. Explain 'Natal' means related to birth/Christmas, which is why he used it near 25 December. After this, the Cape became a regular stop for passing ships - which leads to the Dutch. [SME] Confirm the naming of Natal and the 1497-1498 dates against SAHO before production.

slides.title: Meeting at the Cape
slides.lead: When sailors and the Khoikhoi met, there was both trade and, at times, conflict.

blocks.text: Passing ships needed fresh water, meat and rest. Sailors sometimes **traded** metal and beads with the Khoikhoi for cattle and sheep. But the two sides did not share a language, and misunderstandings could turn dangerous.

items: Trade helped tired sailors get fresh food for the long journey.
In 1510 a fight broke out at Table Bay and a Portuguese leader and many of his men were killed.
For many years after that, most Portuguese ships chose not to stop at the Cape.

media.alt: Two groups of people facing each other on a beach, one beside a boat, exchanging goods.
media.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 240 160' role='img'><rect width='240' height='160' fill='#f4efe6'/><rect y='118' width='240' height='42' fill='#c8b89a'/><rect y='100' width='240' height='18' fill='#3a6b8a'/><g fill='#2f5233'><circle cx='70' cy='70' r='10'/><rect x='63' y='82' width='14' height='30'/><circle cx='98' cy='72' r='10'/><rect x='91' y='84' width='14' height='28'/></g><g fill='#7a5a3a'><circle cx='160' cy='70' r='10'/><rect x='153' y='82' width='14' height='30'/><circle cx='188' cy='72' r='10'/><rect x='181' y='84' width='14' height='28'/></g><rect x='118' y='86' width='6' height='6' fill='#e8a13a'/><path d='M206 100 q16 -6 30 0 v10 h-30 Z' fill='#5c3f26'/></svg>

slides.notes: Handle this sensitively and honestly at Grade 6 level. Contact had two sides: peaceful trade AND conflict. The 1510 clash at Table Bay (in which the Portuguese Viceroy Francisco de Almeida and many men died) is why the Portuguese largely avoided the Cape afterwards. Avoid blaming one side; explain that no common language and different customs made trust hard. [SME] Please verify the 1510 Table Bay event, the identity of the leader, and casualty wording before production - keep it age-appropriate.

slides.title: The Dutch and the VOC
slides.lead: More than a hundred years later, traders from the Netherlands needed a stop halfway to the East.

blocks.text: The **Dutch East India Company**, known by its Dutch letters **VOC**, sent many ships between Europe and the East. The Cape sat right in the middle of that long journey.

items: Sailors on long voyages often became sick without fresh food and water.
The VOC wanted a **refreshment station** at the Cape to grow vegetables and get fresh meat.
So the Company decided to send people to build a base at Table Bay.

media.alt: A trading ship flag beside crates and barrels, showing a company trading post.
media.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 220 160' role='img'><rect width='220' height='160' fill='#f4efe6'/><rect x='40' y='30' width='6' height='100' fill='#5c3f26'/><path d='M46 32 h60 v34 h-60 Z' fill='#e8a13a'/><text x='76' y='55' font-size='16' text-anchor='middle' fill='#5c1414'>VOC</text><g fill='#7a5a3a'><rect x='120' y='96' width='36' height='34'/><rect x='158' y='104' width='30' height='26'/></g><g stroke='#5c3f26' stroke-width='2'><line x1='120' y1='113' x2='156' y2='113'/><line x1='138' y1='96' x2='138' y2='130'/></g><ellipse cx='176' cy='104' rx='15' ry='6' fill='#8a6a34'/></svg>

slides.notes: Bridge from the Portuguese to the Dutch. Stress the reason: the VOC was a big trading company whose ships took months to reach the East, and sailors fell ill (often from scurvy, from lack of fresh food). A halfway 'refreshment station' at the Cape would supply fresh vegetables, water and meat. This is the reason the Dutch came - trade and supply, not at first to build a country.

slides.title: Jan van Riebeeck at the Cape
slides.lead: In 1652 the VOC sent Jan van Riebeeck to set up the refreshment station.

blocks.text: **Jan van Riebeeck** arrived at Table Bay in April 1652 with a small group of people. They built a fort for safety and planted a large vegetable garden to feed the passing ships.

items: They grew vegetables and fruit and kept animals for fresh meat.
They traded with the Khoikhoi for cattle and sheep.
This small station slowly grew - it became the start of a permanent European settlement.

media.alt: A star-shaped fort next to neat rows of a vegetable garden below a flat-topped mountain.
media.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 240 160' role='img'><rect width='240' height='160' fill='#f4efe6'/><path d='M20 60 h120 l16 -22 h44 v42 H20 Z' fill='#9aa77f'/><rect y='96' width='240' height='64' fill='#7a8b5a'/><g stroke='#3f6b45' stroke-width='3'><line x1='30' y1='108' x2='150' y2='108'/><line x1='30' y1='122' x2='150' y2='122'/><line x1='30' y1='136' x2='150' y2='136'/></g><path d='M176 104 l14 -8 l14 8 l-4 22 h-20 Z' fill='#c8b89a' stroke='#8a6a34' stroke-width='2'/><rect x='186' y='114' width='8' height='12' fill='#7a5a3a'/></svg>

slides.notes: This is the turning point for the lesson's second half. Van Riebeeck arrived in April 1652 with about ninety people; they built a fort and the Company's Garden to supply ships. Emphasise the phrase 'permanent settlement' - unlike the Portuguese who only stopped, the Dutch stayed. That permanence is what makes 1652 so important and leads to the consequences on the next slide. [SME] Confirm the arrival month and the approximate size of van Riebeeck's party (often given as around 90) before production.

slides.eyebrow: What it meant for the Khoikhoi
slides.title: Change for the people of the Cape
slides.lead: The new settlement slowly took land and grazing that the Khoikhoi depended on.

blocks.text: At first the Dutch and the Khoikhoi traded with each other. But as the settlement grew, the Dutch wanted **more land** for farms - the same land the Khoikhoi used to graze their cattle.

items: The Khoikhoi lost grazing land and, over time, many of their cattle.
Disagreements over land and cattle led to fighting between the two sides.
Life for the Khoikhoi and San was changed forever by the settlement at the Cape.

media.alt: A fence line splitting green grazing land, with cattle on one side and a farmhouse on the other.
media.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 240 160' role='img'><rect width='240' height='160' fill='#f4efe6'/><rect y='96' width='240' height='64' fill='#7a8b5a'/><g stroke='#6b6152' stroke-width='3'><line x1='120' y1='60' x2='120' y2='150'/><line x1='104' y1='84' x2='136' y2='84'/><line x1='104' y1='104' x2='136' y2='104'/></g><g fill='#5c3a1f'><ellipse cx='56' cy='112' rx='18' ry='9'/><rect x='44' y='112' width='3' height='12'/><rect x='66' y='112' width='3' height='12'/></g><g fill='#7a5a3a'><rect x='168' y='96' width='40' height='30'/></g><path d='M166 96 l22 -16 l22 16 Z' fill='#8a1f1f'/></svg>

slides.notes: Deliver the main consequence clearly and fairly. The relationship began with trade but changed as settlers wanted farmland - the very grazing land the Khoikhoi needed for their herds. This led to loss of land, loss of cattle, and conflict. Keep it factual and age-appropriate; the point is that arrival of the settlement permanently changed Khoikhoi and San life. This connects to later CAPS topics on colonisation of the Cape.

slides.title: Key dates: 1488 to 1652

blocks.alt: A timeline from 1488 to 1652 showing Dias rounding the Cape, da Gama reaching India, the 1510 clash at Table Bay, and van Riebeeck arriving in 1652.

events.label: Vasco da Gama sails on to India
Clash with the Khoikhoi at Table Bay
Dutch VOC ships pass the Cape often
Van Riebeeck starts the refreshment station

slides.notes: Use this slide to help learners sequence events, a key CAPS Intermediate Phase skill. Ask them to notice the long gap between the first Portuguese voyages (late 1400s) and the Dutch settlement (1652) - more than 150 years. The Portuguese found and used the sea route; the Dutch were the ones who finally settled at the Cape.

slides.title: Two sides of the same story
slides.lead: The same voyages look very different depending on who you are.

items: A sea route to the riches of the East was opened.
Trade and ships grew, and the Cape became an important stop.
The Dutch built a lasting base at the tip of Africa.
Strangers arrived on land that was already their home.
They lost grazing land and cattle as the settlement grew.
Their way of life was changed forever.

slides.notes: This is the thinking slide. Teach 'point of view' - the same history feels like an exciting achievement from Europe's side and a loss from the Khoisan side. Encourage learners to hold both truths at once. This models balanced historical thinking and prepares them for the review questions.

slides.title: What we learned
slides.lead: Portuguese sailors found the sea route around southern Africa, and the Dutch later settled at the Cape - changing life for the Khoisan.

blocks.text: Remember the story: Europeans wanted a sea road to the East; **Dias** rounded the Cape in 1488 and **da Gama** reached India by 1498; and in **1652 van Riebeeck** built the refreshment station that grew into a lasting settlement.

blocks.question: Why did the Portuguese want to sail around the tip of Africa?
Name the two famous Portuguese explorers in this lesson and say what each one did.
How did the arrival of the Dutch settlement at the Cape change life for the Khoikhoi?

slides.notes: Close by recapping the three key moments: Dias (1488), da Gama (1497-98) and van Riebeeck (1652). Use the three questions as written work or class discussion; the marks follow CAPS style. The last question checks that learners can explain a consequence, not just recall a fact. Preview the next unit: the colonisation of the Cape in the 17th and 18th centuries.
